author: @bodymindresiliencechannel
text: Really exciting.  This is going to need anchoring down big time, because the Darwin collector inevitably pushes up when it pushes the air down, and it's a big kite.  The advantage of fitting a rotor/generator on top is that the collector is pushed down and you have far less likelihood it will end up joining Dorothy in the Wizard of Oz.  Similarly, that automatically leads to a propellor-type design - otherwise there is still a cap on top where the air is diverted by 90 degrees that will be pushed upwards - so one way or another this frame will need to be attached to ground anchors (or for a small one, a heavy concrete block).  A light hyperbolic shape might be achievable using a canvas membrane held (in zero wind) as a cone between two rings?? (rather than a solid shutter collector).  Of course, that's even more like a kite.  I am trying to get a sense of the effect in air - which is slightly different from water, being compressible - allowing it to pressurise (and so stop flow) or form a vacuum (and so encourage flow).  The hyperbolic forms tend to spiral motion and vacuum, and so anything that has those forms will also tend to vacuum and spirals - so the 2D modelling (even if it's radial 2D modelling) is probably substantially underestimating the flow as the fan starts to spin.  As such the vertical veins may bring in air directionally and provide mechanical rigidity, but they get in the way of the spiral flow and create an elevated pressure within the intake structure.  The air will also probably respond well to bigger inlet "width" (so it doesn't compress so much) but that automatically results in a taller tower.  Lots of compromises.  On the height-width ratio, phi seems to work pretty well in flow situations - for instance chimneying from a dome shaped bread oven is optimised with a structure and door opening height:width ratio of about 5:8.  I'm interested to see the result, because the whole structure and air exhaust may cause a local pressure increase (reducing wind intake) - and it's not immediately obvious what will dominate the flow.  But the journey youre going on is really wonderful to watch, and so much fun, and it's obviously going somewhere interesting and  useful.

author: @alexstewart7652
text: Another great video; whilst not being an engineer of any sort, just thought it may be of interest to set an ‘end goal’, mine being that it would be fitted into a U.K. chimney pot/ stack, as there are so many of them, mostly redundant, with the chimney being used for the services such batteries, cables etc. I appreciate that there will be many flaws in this thought, eg any servicing required for the bits at the top. Also as the common older houses had at least two chimneys together, then the’waste/exhaust air could be diverted out. Also thought this maybe interesting as it may provide a nominal scale for the items at the top, and then help to work out what power could be produced. Am sure plenty will say that this bonkers, that’s fine, it’s just a thought for applications in our inefficient, cold housing stock.

text: "All computer simulation are wrong but a few are useful"  I think this is a useful one, mainly because it indicates wind speed; where the power is!  One half the wind speed equals one eighth the power.  The highest wind speed is at the top of the collector there the wind is trying to get around it.  The simulation shows the air at the the turbine much slower than the wind outside.

author: @11Sam11
text: Rob. I saw this issue arising a mile away. That's why i strongly suggested to you from your previous videos of using a fluidic type diodes such as a tesla valve or an equivalent to mitigate aerodynamic losses  towards the turbine blades and improving diodicity levels greatly. If you 3d print design a tesla valve on the crossed section area of the fin blades (flat sided not at the edge points) on your darwin template or an inward column positioned between inlet funnel to further increase more pressure flow,  it will forcefully capture the wind convergently  in one direction minimising wind dissiptitation. Here's an article that demonstrates this with different valveless models types using computational field dynamics analysis performing on wind and water turbines. "A Study on Fluidic Diode for Wave Energy Conversion-Effect of Bypass Geometry on the Turbine Performance" I'm truly excited to be a part of this extraordinary journey. looking forward to your next video.

Honestly I would do it in steps and do the Waters turbine with a parabolic cone first and then look at the tower flow after as I expect it will have less improvement.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Wonder if it makes sense to consider the "exhaust air" out of these turbines?  Since you are taking energy out, the air presumably will be moving slower as it exits the machine.  It would seem that would build up pressure at the output end, and so it would be slightly more difficult for more air to come along and fill that space.  A conical shroud around the exhaust, allowing the air to expand might help.  Just speculating; thinking out loud, but it seems consideration of the exhaust is an important part of improving the efficiency.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: If you google Airfoil Tools you will find some good information.  Each airfoil has an Airfoil detail button that will bring up graphs showing that airfoils performance at different Reynolds numbers.  The Lift to Drag ratio (Cl/Cd v Alpha) graph is a good one to study.  It will show the angle of attack where the airfoil is  most efficient ( highest lift to drag ratio).  On the home page in the upper left corner under Applications is a Reynolds number calculator. To get a higher Reynolds number when all else are equal the chord length can be increased.

author: @npsit1
text: I'm sure there are numerous options for coupling a darwin collector to a turbine of any kind. Is it better to direct and restrict the air to a small section of the turbine to increase the wind speed - either from the inside or outside? Are tall or short turbine blades better? Should the air be directed perpendicular to the blades or at an angle? Should the angle be acute or obtuse to the angle of the blades? So many experiments.

author: @Howtosolveproblems
text: Neat. Very nice. So, am I reading this correctly  - we have a broad region of upwind flow entering on the left hand, inlet side, this is accelerated to a narrow region of high speed flow leading into the mechanism at the centre, which splits into a similarly narrow region of high speed flow to the bottom right and a broad region of low speed flow at the top right on the downwind, right hand, exit side of the device, in deep blue? So, is the downwind, deep blue, exit side essentially stationary flow, or does this illustration indeed indicate leakage out of the downwind side? If so, how large is that leakage? What are the respective volume flow rates encompassed by each of these locations? What proportion of the mass entering into the device on the left ultimately leaks from the device on the right hand, exit side? This is really interesting stuff, but it's a little hard for me to judge only from the velocity field itself and the relative areas through which these flows, flow.
